title: Missing EINTR retry in blocking I/O syscall wrapper
message: Blocking I/O syscall without EINTR retry loop. Signals (SIGCHLD, SIGWINCH, etc.) can interrupt the call, returning -1 with errno=EINTR. Without a retry, the caller treats this as a real error — dropping connections, corrupting state, or losing data.
remediation: Wrap in: do { n = syscall(...); } while (n < 0 && errno == EINTR); — Only retry on EINTR, not on other errors. For send(), also use MSG_NOSIGNAL to prevent SIGPIPE from killing the process.

title: Terminal dimensions used without zero/negative check
message: Terminal dimensions from TIOCGWINSZ can be 0x0 (expect PTYs, detached terminals). Using (- rows 1) with rows=0 produces -1, which causes infinite loops in do/for constructs with = termination tests.
remediation: Always validate: (let ([cols (if (<= cols 0) 80 cols)] [rows (if (<= rows 0) 24 rows)]) ...). Apply at both the protocol boundary (where dimensions are received) and the rendering boundary (defense in depth).

title: Daemon process does not ensure fds 0/1/2 are open
message: After daemonization (setsid + fork + close-all-fds), fds 0/1/2 are closed. New files/sockets get assigned these fd numbers, causing Chez port I/O to go to the wrong fd (e.g., display writes to a socket instead of /dev/null).
remediation: Call ffi_ensure_std_fds() immediately after daemonization to open /dev/null on any closed standard fds. This reserves fd numbers 0-2 and prevents collisions with application fds.

title: do-loop termination bound derived from unchecked input
message: do-loop with = termination test where the bound comes from arithmetic on external input. If the bound becomes negative, the incrementing counter never hits it, causing an infinite loop.
remediation: Either validate the input (max 1 value) before computing the bound, or use >= instead of = for the termination test when the bound could be negative.

title: Using OpenSSL AEAD mode for OpenSSH ChaCha20-Poly1305
message: OpenSSL's EVP_chacha20_poly1305() implements RFC 8439 AEAD (padded AAD, appended lengths) which is INCOMPATIBLE with OpenSSH's chacha20-poly1305@openssh.com (raw Poly1305 over concatenated data). Packets will encrypt/decrypt locally but the server rejects them with 'message authentication code incorrect'. Kex succeeds because NEWKEYS is sent unencrypted — the bug only manifests on the first encrypted packet.
remediation: Use EVP_chacha20() for encryption + EVP_MAC_fetch(NULL, "POLY1305", NULL) for raw MAC. Generate poly key from ChaCha20(K2, nonce, counter=0), encrypt payload with counter=1, compute MAC over raw (enc_length || enc_payload) without padding or length suffixes.

title: Using 0 as sentinel for optional integer record field
message: Record field initialized to 0 with (> field 0) as 'has value' check. Fails when 0 is a valid value (e.g., server-assigned channel ID 0, fd 0, port 0). Causes infinite loops in dispatch-until or poll loops that wait for the field to be set.
remediation: Initialize optional integer fields to #f and check with (field ch) for truthiness. #f is never a valid integer, so this correctly distinguishes 'not yet set' from 'set to 0'.

title: PTY master fd closed in multiple code paths
message: PTY master fd may be closed by both the PTY EOF handler (in handle-pty-output!) and the cleanup function (sm-cleanup-dead-panes!). Double-closing an fd can close an unrelated fd if the number was reused between closes.
remediation: Use guard (e [#t (void)]) around all ffi-stream-close calls on master fds. Alternatively, set the fd to -1 after closing and check before closing. The cleanup function should be the single owner of close responsibility.

title: SSH/mux password passed via environment variable
message: Passing passwords via environment variables (e.g., _JSH_MUX_PW) is safer than command-line args (not visible in ps output) but the env var persists in /proc/<pid>/environ until explicitly cleared.
remediation: Clear the env var immediately after reading it in the child process: (putenv "_JSH_MUX_PW" ""). The parent should also clear it after fork-exec. Verify the var is cleared by checking /proc/<pid>/environ.

title: with-timeout tick count allows CPU exhaustion
message: with-timeout with very large tick count (>10M) effectively disables the timeout. An attacker supplying the timeout value can cause CPU exhaustion. The engine system uses ticks, not wall-clock time — ticks/second varies by workload.
remediation: Cap the timeout seconds parameter: (with-timeout (min user-timeout MAX-TIMEOUT) default thunk). Use a maximum of 30-60 seconds for user-facing operations. For internal use, document the expected bounds.

title: foreign-alloc without matching foreign-free
message: foreign-alloc allocates unmanaged C memory. Without a matching foreign-free, the memory is leaked — Chez's GC does NOT track or free foreign-allocated memory. In long-running services, this leads to unbounded memory growth.
remediation: Always pair foreign-alloc with foreign-free in a dynamic-wind or unwind-protect: (let ([ptr (foreign-alloc size)]) (dynamic-wind void (lambda () (use ptr)) (lambda () (foreign-free ptr)))). For ftype-pointer, free via (foreign-free (ftype-pointer-address p)).

title: ftype-set! without bounds validation
message: ftype-set! writes directly to foreign memory without bounds checking. Writing past the allocated size corrupts memory, potentially leading to code execution. Chez's ftype system trusts the programmer to stay within bounds.
remediation: Always allocate with ftype-sizeof to ensure correct size. When working with arrays, validate indices. Use ftype-pointer-null? to check for null pointers before dereferencing.

title: FASL deserialization of untrusted data
message: FASL deserialization uses Chez's read procedure internally, which can construct arbitrary Scheme objects. Deserializing data from untrusted sources could lead to injection if the data contains executable forms or exploits reader macros.
remediation: Only deserialize FASL data from trusted sources (local cache files, known-good IPC channels). For untrusted data, use JSON or a restricted reader. Validate the deserialized data structure before use.

title: Ephemeron key identity depends on GC timing
message: Ephemeron and weak hash tables use eq? identity. If keys are temporary objects (strings, lists), entries may vanish unpredictably as GC collects the key objects. Only symbols and objects with stable identity are safe as keys.
remediation: Use symbols, records, or long-lived objects as ephemeron keys. If using temporary objects, ensure a strong reference exists elsewhere. For string keys, use make-hashtable with equal-hash/equal? instead.

title: Contract checks add runtime overhead without graceful handling
message: Contract checks (check-argument, define/contract) raise conditions that crash the program if unhandled. In production code, this may be the desired behavior for fail-fast, but in libraries consumed by others it can be surprising.
remediation: Document which functions have contracts. Consider wrapping contract-heavy code paths in guard/try-catch at API boundaries. For performance-critical inner loops, avoid contracts — use them at module boundaries only.

title: Thread barrier with fewer threads than parties causes deadlock
message: If fewer threads call barrier-wait! than the barrier's party count, all waiting threads block forever. If a thread dies before reaching the barrier, all other threads deadlock.
remediation: Ensure the number of threads matches make-barrier's party count exactly. Use barrier-reset! from a supervisor thread if a worker dies. Consider adding a timeout wrapper: (with-timeout 30.0 'deadlock (lambda () (barrier-wait! b))).

title: Region reference used after with-region scope exits
message: The region module allocates memory via foreign-alloc and frees it in dynamic-wind cleanup when with-region exits. Any region-ref or region-set! on a pointer from that region after scope exit dereferences freed memory, causing undefined behavior or segfault.
remediation: Ensure all region-ref/region-set! calls are lexically inside the with-region body. Never capture a region pointer in a closure, channel, or continuation that outlives the scope.

title: Movable value accessed after move! invalidated the source
message: move! transfers ownership and invalidates the source. Subsequent access on the original binding returns a runtime error. Interleaving move-ref and move! on the same binding indicates a logic error.
remediation: After move!, never read the source binding again. Restructure so move! is the last operation on any given source.

title: Affine value consumed by affine-use! more than once
message: Affine types enforce use-at-most-once. Second affine-use! raises runtime error, but multiple calls targeting the same binding indicate a design-level logic bug.
remediation: Audit every affine binding to ensure affine-use! is called exactly once. Place affine-use! at exactly one branch exit if conditional.

title: Mutable borrow attempted while shared borrows active
message: The borrow checker requires mutable borrows to be exclusive. Nested borrow/borrow-mut calls indicate mixing read and write access, which the runtime rejects and signals a data-race-prone design.
remediation: Release all shared borrows before acquiring mutable borrow. Serialize writes via mutex, actor, or single-writer epoch.

title: MVCC store read outside transaction may see inconsistent state
message: mvcc-ref outside mvcc-transact does not acquire a snapshot version. Concurrent writers may commit between successive reads, violating snapshot isolation.
remediation: Always perform mvcc-ref inside mvcc-transact for consistent snapshots. Document single-key reads that intentionally skip isolation.

title: World state loaded from untrusted FASL source
message: load-world deserializes FASL data which can reconstruct arbitrary Scheme objects. Loading from untrusted sources is equivalent to arbitrary code execution.
remediation: Only load-world from trusted, application-produced files with appropriate filesystem permissions. Verify integrity with cryptographic signature. Never accept world image path from user input.

title: Unsafe transmute to extend lifetime in FFI handle stores
message: Transmuting a borrowed type to 'static lifetime to store in a static HashMap bypasses Rust's borrow checker. If the owning object (e.g., Connection) is dropped while the transmuted reference (e.g., Statement) still exists, the reference becomes dangling — causing use-after-free.
remediation: Use raw C API pointers instead of Rust wrapper types with lifetimes. For sqlite: use rusqlite::ffi::sqlite3_prepare_v2 etc. directly instead of transmuting Statement<'conn> to Statement<'static>. Keep the Connection alive in the same entry to manage the sqlite3* lifetime.

title: Mutex::lock().unwrap() in FFI can panic across boundary
message: If a previous FFI call panicked while holding the mutex, the mutex becomes poisoned. Subsequent .lock().unwrap() calls will panic, and if not caught by ffi_wrap, this is undefined behavior across the FFI boundary.
remediation: Use .lock().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()) to recover from poisoned mutexes, or ensure all mutex access is within ffi_wrap() which catches panics. The ffi_wrap pattern handles this case but only if it's the outermost catch.

title: SQLite statement not finalized before connection close
message: Closing a SQLite database without first finalizing all prepared statements can cause memory leaks or undefined behavior. The raw sqlite3_close returns SQLITE_BUSY if statements are still active.
remediation: Always finalize all prepared statements before closing the database. Use dynamic-wind or guard to ensure cleanup: (dynamic-wind void (lambda () (use stmt)) (lambda () (sqlite-finalize stmt))). The Rust sqlite module auto-finalizes on close as a safety net, but explicit finalization is preferred.

title: Landlock enforcement is permanent and irreversible
message: Once landlock-enforce! or landlock-restrict-self! is called, filesystem restrictions are PERMANENT for the process and all future children. There is no API to remove restrictions. Accidentally enforcing too-restrictive rules will break the application with no recovery path.
remediation: Test Landlock rules in a forked subprocess first. Always include system paths (/usr, /lib, /etc, /proc, /dev) for read access. Log the exact rules being applied. Consider a dry-run mode that validates paths exist before enforcing. Never call from library code without explicit user opt-in.

title: from_utf8_unchecked on user-provided data
message: std::str::from_utf8_unchecked assumes the input is valid UTF-8. If called with non-UTF-8 bytes from Scheme (e.g., binary data passed as string), it creates an invalid &str which is undefined behavior in Rust.
remediation: Use std::str::from_utf8() with proper error handling for data from external sources. Only use from_utf8_unchecked when the data is known to come from Chez Scheme's string->utf8 which guarantees valid UTF-8.

title: Bare read on untrusted input without read-eval #f
message: Chez Scheme read supports #.(expr) which executes code during parsing. Reading untrusted data without (parameterize ([read-eval #f]) ...) is a remote code execution vulnerability.
remediation: Wrap in (parameterize ([read-eval #f]) (read port)), or use jerboa-read which has built-in depth/size limits. Never use bare read on network, file, or user input.

title: copy-environment from interaction-environment without restrict
message: Copying the full interaction-environment gives sandboxed code access to ALL Chez primitives including system, foreign-procedure, eval, etc. This defeats any sandbox.
remediation: Use (make-restricted-environment) from (std security restrict) instead. It provides an allowlist-only environment with ~60 safe bindings.

title: Empty hosts list in network capability treated as allow-all
message: An empty hosts list in a network capability may be incorrectly treated as "allow all" rather than "deny all". This inverts the security model.
remediation: Empty hosts list should mean deny-all. Use ("*") explicitly to allow all hosts. The fixed net-allowed-host? returns #f for empty host lists.

title: Dangerous operation without taint check on user input
message: Dangerous operation (system, file I/O, delete) called with potentially tainted user input without a taint check.
remediation: Validate and sanitize file paths before use. For system() calls, prefer run-process with an argument list to avoid shell injection. For file operations, verify the path does not escape expected directories.

title: Direct (chezscheme) import in user/application code
message: Direct (chezscheme) import bypasses the capability system and restricted environment. AI-generated code commonly uses this to escape sandboxes.
remediation: Use (jerboa prelude) or specific std modules instead. Run audit-imports-file from (std security import-audit) in CI to catch these. Only trusted infrastructure modules should import (chezscheme) directly.

title: sanitize-html used in HTML attribute context
message: sanitize-html only escapes < > & " ' — sufficient for element content but NOT for attribute values. Attribute breakout is possible with backtick, equals, slash characters.
remediation: Use sanitize-html-attribute for attribute values. Use sanitize-url-attribute for href/src/action attributes (also blocks javascript: scheme).

title: FASL deserialization in privilege separation channel
message: The privsep channel uses fasl-write/fasl-read for serialization. While pipes are local, a compromised child could send crafted FASL data to the supervisor.
remediation: Consider adding message validation in the supervisor handler. Validate the structure of deserialized messages before processing. The privsep channel is pipe-based (not network) so the risk is lower.

title: Seccomp/Landlock stub used in production without enforcement
message: seccomp-install! and landlock-install! are currently stubs that record policy but do NOT enforce it. Using them creates a false sense of security.
remediation: These functions now print a WARNING to stderr. Do not rely on them for actual sandboxing until real kernel enforcement is implemented. Use privsep + restricted environments for actual isolation.

title: Fork without child process limit
message: Forking without tracking child count can lead to fork bombs or zombie accumulation.
remediation: Use make-privsep from (std security privsep) which enforces *max-privsep-children* (default 64) and tracks PIDs for proper reaping via waitpid.

title: Path comparison without canonicalization
message: Comparing paths with string-prefix? without first canonicalizing via realpath allows symlink and .. traversal bypasses.
remediation: Use canonicalize-path from (std security capability) which calls realpath(3) via FFI. Or use safe-path-join from (std security sanitize) which canonicalizes path components.

title: Path traversal in embed/virtual filesystem
message: Path containing '..' components can escape the virtual filesystem boundary. An attacker can request '../../etc/passwd' to read files outside the embed root.
remediation: Reject any path containing '..' components before resolving. Use (string-contains path "..") as a fast check, then canonicalize with realpath and verify the result is still under the embed root. Never rely solely on string-prefix? without canonicalization.

title: Path comparison without symlink resolution enables sandbox escape
message: Using string-prefix? on paths without resolving symlinks first allows symlink-based sandbox escapes. An attacker can create a symlink inside the allowed directory that points outside, bypassing the prefix check.
remediation: Always call realpath() (via FFI or canonicalize-path) on the path BEFORE comparing with string-prefix?. Compare the canonicalized path against the canonicalized sandbox root. This resolves all symlinks and '..' components.

title: load-shared-object crashes static binary at boot
message: Calling load-shared-object at module top level fatally errors in static binaries where no dynamic loader is available. This crashes the entire process during library initialization, before any user code runs.
remediation: Wrap in (or (guard (e [#t #f]) (load-shared-object "lib.so") #t) #t). In static builds, symbols are pre-registered via Sforeign_symbol() so the .so is unnecessary. The guard pattern makes it graceful.

title: Deserializing FASL from untrusted source without validation
message: Raw fasl-read can deserialize arbitrary objects including procedures. An attacker who controls the FASL input can inject executable closures that run when the object is used. This is equivalent to pickle deserialization attacks in Python.
remediation: Never fasl-read data from untrusted sources. FASL is a serialization format for internal use only — treat it like pickle in Python. If you must deserialize external data, use JSON or another safe format. For trusted internal FASL (boot files, caches), ensure the source is integrity-checked (e.g., HMAC or embedded).

title: Resource acquisition without guaranteed cleanup
message: Resource acquired in a let binding without dynamic-wind or with-resource cleanup. If the body raises an exception, the resource is leaked — file descriptors, database connections, or sockets are never closed.
remediation: Use (std resource) with-resource macro for guaranteed cleanup: (with-resource ([db (sqlite-open "test.db")]) body ...). This wraps the body in dynamic-wind and auto-detects the cleanup procedure for common resource types.

title: Using bare (error) instead of structured conditions
message: Bare (error 'who "message") creates an unstructured exception that can only be caught with a catch-all handler. Using structured conditions from (std error conditions) allows callers to pattern-match on error type with guard clauses (e.g., db-error?, network-error?, timeout-error?).
remediation: Use (raise (condition (make-db-query-error 'db 'sqlite sql) (make-message-condition "details"))) or the convenience raisers: (raise-db-error 'sqlite "query failed: ~a" sql). Import (std error conditions).

title: SQL query built with string-append instead of parameterized query
message: SQL query constructed via string-append or format with user input is vulnerable to SQL injection. An attacker can terminate the string and inject arbitrary SQL commands.
remediation: Use parameterized queries: (sqlite-execute db "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?" user-id). Never interpolate user input into SQL strings. The (std safe) module's safe-sqlite-query wraps this pattern.

title: Module availability flag set before all evals complete
message: Availability flag set to #t before subsequent eval calls. If a later eval fails, the flag remains #t but function bindings are still #f, causing 'attempt to apply non-procedure' at runtime.
remediation: Move (set! *-available? #t) to AFTER all (eval ...) calls succeed. Also reset to #f in the guard handler.

title: SQL query built via string-append or format
message: SQL query built via string concatenation instead of parameterized queries. This is a SQL injection vector — user input could modify query structure.
remediation: Use parameterized queries: (sqlite-query db "SELECT * FROM t WHERE id = ?" user-id) instead of (sqlite-query db (string-append "SELECT * FROM t WHERE id = " user-id))

title: Direct import of raw FFI module without safe wrapper
message: Direct import of raw FFI module bypasses contract checks, resource tracking, and SQL safety. Use (std safe) or (jerboa prelude safe) for safe wrappers with argument validation, guardian-based leak detection, and injection heuristics.
remediation: Replace (import (std db sqlite-native)) with (import (std safe)) or (import (jerboa prelude safe)).

title: TLS server created without client certificate verification
message: TLS server context created without client certificate verification (no mTLS). Any client with network access can connect. If the server handles privileged operations (shell access, file I/O, database), unauthenticated TLS is insufficient — an attacker can connect and issue commands.
remediation: Use rustls-server-ctx-new-mtls instead, providing a CA cert to verify client certificates. For self-signed setups, use the same cert as both server cert and client CA: (rustls-server-ctx-new-mtls cert key cert).

title: cap_enter() called without restricting file descriptors
message: Entering Capsicum capability mode without first restricting fd rights. All open fds retain full rights — a compromised process can read/write/seek/mmap on any inherited fd. cap_enter() alone only prevents opening NEW fds; existing fds are unrestricted.
remediation: Use capsicum-apply-preset! (which restricts fds then enters cap mode) or call capsicum-limit-fd! on each open fd before capsicum-enter!. Minimum: restrict stdin to read, stdout/stderr to write.

title: TLS server context created without corresponding free
message: TLS server context handle allocated but may not be freed. Each context holds Rust-allocated memory (cert chains, key material, verifier state). Without rustls-server-ctx-free, this memory leaks for the process lifetime.
remediation: Always pair with rustls-server-ctx-free in a cleanup/finally path: (dynamic-wind void (lambda () ...) (lambda () (rustls-server-ctx-free ctx))). For long-lived servers, create the context once and free on shutdown.

title: call-with-output-file silently fails on existing files (R6RS)
message: call-with-output-file raises an exception if the file already exists (R6RS default behavior). This causes data loss if the caller expects overwrite semantics — the write silently fails and the user's data is not saved.
remediation: Use (open-output-file path 'replace) with explicit close-port, or (with-output-to-file path thunk 'replace). The 'replace option is Chez-specific and safely overwrites existing files.

title: Async callback writes to wrong Scintilla document after buffer switch
message: Async callback uses qt-current-editor at callback time, not at registration time. If the user switches buffers during the async operation, set-text! writes into the wrong Scintilla document, causing data corruption (file content written into terminal doc or vice versa).
remediation: Capture the target buffer's doc pointer at registration time. In the callback, temporarily switch to the target document via SCI_SETDOCPOINTER before writing, then switch back. See recipe scintilla-async-document-safety.

title: qt-app-process-events! can mutate state mid-operation (re-entrancy)
message: qt-app-process-events! pumps the Qt event queue, which can fire key event callbacks. A queued switch-buffer keystroke will change the editor's active document mid-operation, causing subsequent writes to corrupt the wrong buffer. Any mutable state (window-buffer associations, editor document pointers) may change.
remediation: After calling qt-app-process-events!, re-validate all mutable state before using it. Re-check window-buffer associations, document pointers, and buffer identity. See recipe qt-process-events-recheck-state.

title: Hardcoded character pixel width in terminal column calculation
message: Terminal column count calculated with hardcoded character width (7 or 8 pixels). At non-default font sizes, this produces incorrect column counts — terminal apps format output for the wrong width, causing lines to wrap or truncate incorrectly. Can cause information disclosure if sensitive data wraps onto visible lines unexpectedly.
remediation: Use SCI_TEXTWIDTH (2275) to measure actual character width: (let ((char-w (max 1 (sci-send/string ed 2275 \"0\" 0)))) (quotient widget-w char-w)). See recipe scintilla-measure-char-width.

title: Using u8* FFI type for foreign-alloc'd output pointers
message: foreign-procedure with u8* parameter may be incorrect for output pointer buffers. u8* expects a Scheme bytevector — passing a foreign-alloc'd pointer (fixnum) causes 'invalid foreign-procedure argument' at runtime. Use void* for output pointer parameters that receive foreign-alloc'd buffers.
remediation: Review each u8* parameter: if the caller passes (foreign-alloc N) to it, change the type to void*. Keep u8* only for parameters that receive actual bytevectors from (string->utf8) or (make-bytevector).

title: Qt parent-child double-free: explicit child destroy after parent destroy
message: Two qt-widget-destroy! calls in close proximity may double-free a Qt child widget. Qt automatically destroys all children when a parent QObject/QWidget is destroyed. Explicitly destroying a child after its parent has been destroyed causes a double-free SIGSEGV (appears as QBindingStorage::clear() crash in Qt 6).
remediation: Before destroying a parent widget, set the child variable to #f so any subsequent conditional destroy is skipped. Example: (set! container #f) before (qt-widget-destroy! parent-spl). Guard all child destroy calls with (when child ...) and clear the variable before destroying the parent.

title: Subprocess openssl dgst for cryptographic operations
message: Calling `openssl dgst` via subprocess for SHA256/HMAC is unsafe: (1) vulnerable to PATH hijacking if attacker controls PATH, (2) fails in static binaries where subprocess spawning may be disabled, (3) leaks HMAC key material as a command-line argument visible in /proc, (4) high overhead for bulk operations.
remediation: Use (std crypto native) instead: (native-digest 'sha256 data) for hashing, (native-hmac-sha256 key data) for HMAC. Both accept strings or bytevectors. For static builds, ensure (std crypto native) is included in the WPO boot file list.

title: SRE char-range (/ #\\! #\\~) includes @ allowing email validation bypass
message: The character range (/ #\\! #\\~) covers ASCII 33-126 which includes @ (64). When used in email local-part patterns, this allows double-@ addresses like user@domain@example.com to match, bypassing email validation.
remediation: Use explicit character lists instead of broad ASCII ranges: (or alnum \".\" \"_\" \"+\" \"-\"). If a range is needed, split around @: (or (/ #\\! #\\?) (/ #\\A #\\~))

title: Legacy OpenSSL foreign-procedure call (should use ring via jerboa_native)
message: Direct OpenSSL libcrypto FFI call detected. All crypto operations (CSPRNG, PBKDF2, AES-GCM) should use Rust ring via libjerboa_native instead: jerboa_random_bytes, jerboa_pbkdf2_derive, jerboa_aead_seal, jerboa_aead_open.
remediation: Replace OpenSSL foreign-procedure calls with ring equivalents: RAND_bytes → jerboa_random_bytes, PKCS5_PBKDF2_HMAC → jerboa_pbkdf2_derive, EVP_*encrypt*/EVP_*decrypt* → jerboa_aead_seal/jerboa_aead_open. See cookbook recipes rust-aes-256-gcm-ffi and rust-pbkdf2-ffi.

title: run-safe-eval called from potential fiber context
message: run-safe-eval uses fork() internally. Calling fork() from within a fiber (e.g., an HTTP handler, transducer lambda, or channel callback) silently corrupts the fiber runtime scheduler — workers stop processing events with no error message. This is safe ONLY from top-level OS threads (e.g., actors spawned with spawn-actor, or fork-thread workers).
remediation: Use restricted-eval-string + with-timeout instead. with-timeout uses Chez engines (cooperative preemption, no fork) and is safe from any fiber or thread. See recipe: restricted-eval-fiber-safe-timeout.

title: Catch-all guard returning truthy value silently masks failures
message: A guard clause [#t <truthy-value>] catches ALL exceptions and returns a non-error value (void, #t, #f). When this wraps a procedure call whose result is checked by the caller for errors (e.g., a worker retry machinery doing (ok? result) or (err? result)), all failures silently appear as successes. The caller's error path never fires: no retries, no dead-letter, no alerts.
remediation: For filters/classifiers where permissive-on-error is intentional, document it explicitly: ; permissive: treat eval errors as pass. For handlers called by retry machinery, remove the catch-all guard entirely — let exceptions propagate to the worker's (try (ok (handler evt)) (catch (e) (err e))) so retry logic fires normally.

title: jerbuild wrap-mutable-exports emits duplicate define-syntax for set!-reassigned functions
message: A (set! name ...) at module top-level reassigns a name that may also be wrapped by jerbuild's wrap-mutable-exports pass. If name is also defined as (def (name ...) ...) in the same module, jerbuild will emit both (define name ...) and (define-syntax name ...) — a duplicate definition that causes a compile error in the generated .sls.
remediation: In jerbuild's wrap-mutable-exports pass, collect all (set! name ...) targets from the body and add them to the skip list alongside locally-function-vars. Do not emit a define-syntax wrapper for any name that is both locally defined as a function AND appears as a set! target. See recipe jerbuild-set-reassignment-mutable-export-guard for the corrected implementation.

title: Long-running thread loop without guard — silent death on any exception
message: A fork-thread body containing a let loop with no guard around the loop iteration. Any unhandled exception — including a (cdr #f) from a missing assv lookup, a failed channel-put, or a network error — will kill the thread permanently and silently. For infrastructure threads (Raft message loop, apply fibers, accept loops), a dead thread is catastrophic: the node stops processing, elections stall, and there is no error output.
remediation: Wrap the loop body (or the message-dispatch call) in (guard (exn [#t (void)]) ...) so exceptions are swallowed and the loop continues. For loops that read from a channel and dispatch to a handler, guard the handler call: (guard (exn [#t (void)]) (handle-message! node msg)). For per-iteration side effects, guard each effectful call individually so one bad peer/entry cannot abort processing of all others.
